title: fs_core_simd reads bytes twice: once via SIMD v128_load and again as individual bytes for the hash
description: In the wasm32 fs_core_simd inner loop, each chunk of 16 bytes is loaded as a v128 for the stats path, then the same 16 bytes are re-read individually via d[i..i+15] to build the four u32 words for the hash. This doubles memory traffic for the hash portion on the 4× most common code path. The four u32 words could be extracted from the already-loaded v128 using i32x4_extract_lane instead of four separate from_le_bytes calls.

title: fs_core_simd_exact inner byte loop defeats SIMD by processing hash serially inside the vectorised chunk loop
description: fs_core_simd_exact (the production kernel) loads each 16-byte chunk with v128_load for the stats, but then iterates a scalar for k in 0..16 loop inside the same chunk iteration to apply byte-wise FNV. This serial dependency chain of 16 successive XOR+MUL operations within each vectorised chunk is the bottleneck the comment attributes to the JS version. The SIMD stats gain is paid for by still running the same serial FNV that makes the JS slow. A word-level approximation (as used in fs_core_simd) or batched scalar extraction would break the chain.

title: apply_look clones the full-resolution rgb16 buffer on every texture/clarity edit
description: When texture or clarity is nonzero, apply_look calls rgb16_src.to_vec() to produce a mutable copy before in-place sharpening. For a 20 MP image this is a ~120 MB allocation on the slider hot path. LookRenderer.render() has the same pattern at line 1586. The code comments acknowledge the issue for LookRenderer but it is unresolved for the apply_look path, which is the stateless (non-LookRenderer) call path. A possible mitigation is to provide a caller-owned scratch buffer or to limit the sharpening step to a downscaled proxy.

title: take_rgba allocates a new full-resolution RGBA buffer by copying from the rgb field instead of converting in-place
description: ProcessResult::take_rgba calls rgb_to_rgba on the result of std::mem::take(&mut self.rgb), which first moves the Vec<u8> out of the struct (OK), but rgb_to_rgba then allocates a brand-new Vec<u8> of 4/3 the size and copies into it, leaving the original buffer immediately dropped. For a 20 MP image the original rgb buffer is ~60 MB and the output is ~80 MB; both exist simultaneously. An in-place interleave (shifting bytes from the right) would halve peak memory use.

title: decode_orf_raw always runs full MHC demosaic AND planar-bilinear demosaic, paying double demosaic cost every call
description: decode_orf_raw runs demosaic_rggb_planar (for the fast preview path, lines 628-631) and then unconditionally also runs demosaic_rggb_mhc (for the full quality path, line 672). When only OUT_LIGHTBOX | OUT_THUMB is needed (no OUT_FULL_RGB8), the expensive MHC demosaic is wasted. The output_flags are passed to process_orf_impl, not to decode_orf_raw, so there is no early-exit. Splitting the decode function or passing flags through would avoid the redundant full-res MHC demosaic for preview-only callers.

title: rows_to_json builds JSON via repeated String::push_str with many format! allocations per row
description: rows_to_json constructs the JSON output by calling out.push_str(&format!(...)) for every field of every row. Each format! call allocates a temporary String that is immediately copied then dropped. For a 30-file Gobabeb run (30 rows × ~15 fields = ~450 format! allocations) this is harmless in absolute terms since it runs once, but it is structurally a string-concat-in-loop pattern. Using a serde_json serializer or a single write! to a BufWriter would eliminate all intermediate allocations. This is a benchmark tool, so severity is low.

title: OUT_FULL_16 packs the full-resolution rgb16 into a new Vec<u8> before the rgb16 buffer is used for tone, causing a peak of three concurrent large buffers
description: process_orf_impl packs the full 16-bit buffer into rgb16_full (a new Vec<u8> ~6 bytes/pixel) at line 743-747 before the subsequent tonemapping step, which also reads rgb16 and emits rgb8 (~3 bytes/pixel). For a 20 MP image, rgb16 (~120 MB), rgb16_full (~120 MB), and rgb8 (~60 MB) all coexist. Reordering to pack rgb16_full after drop(rgb16) (which already occurs at line 800) would reduce peak by ~120 MB, or packing and dropping rgb16 before tone would save ~60 MB depending on path.

title: demosaic_bench_equal and demosaic_bench_first_diff both run two full demosaic passes each call, used from bench tooling but exported as always-available WASM API
description: demosaic_bench_equal (line 52) calls demosaic_rggb and demosaic_rggb_simd and compares the results, while demosaic_bench_first_diff (line 59) does the same. Each runs two full demosaic operations on the bench buffer. These are bench-only exports but they are compiled into every production WASM build (no #[cfg] guard), contributing to code-size and making them callable from any JS consumer at any time. Similar patterns exist for the planar variants (lines 97-118). Guarding with a compile-time feature flag would avoid the code-size cost in production.

title: bench_jxl_encode_with_ch creates a new Encoder (and its internal thread pool) on every run iteration
description: The inner bench loop (0..RUNS) calls Encoder::with_threads on each iteration. Each Encoder construction likely initialises a thread pool or at least per-call metadata. The measured hot path is the encode call itself, but the allocation and setup of the Encoder leaks into timing if construction is non-trivial. Moving Encoder construction outside the timed region (before the loop) and reusing it across RUNS would give cleaner measurements and avoid unnecessary overhead.

title: pack_rgb16_full is a pure interleaved u16 → packed-LE-u8 loop with no SIMD; it runs on every OUT_FULL_16 request for full-res images
description: pack_rgb16_full iterates pixel-by-pixel over the full-resolution rgb16 buffer (potentially 20 MP = 60 M u16 reads → 120 M u8 writes) using a scalar loop with write_rgb16_le. On wasm32 this could use v128 loads of two pixels (6 u16 = 12 bytes = ~1 v128) and repack with i8x16_shuffle to produce the 6-byte LE output. On native x86 the compiler may auto-vectorise but the shuffle pattern is non-trivial and manual SIMD would guarantee it. Given this is the M3 16-bit export path (pyramid big levels), throughput matters.

title: Benchmark harness has no performance-regression CI integration; results are written to a local JSON file only
description: results_native.json is written to a local path (benchmark/results_native.json) with no automated comparison against a baseline or any CI step that would catch regressions. The harness already has the structure (generated_at, runsPerFile, rows with per-stage ms) to support a simple threshold check. Adding a CI job that runs the bench on a fixed corpus and compares key metrics (decompressMs, demosaicMs, tonemapMs) against a committed baseline JSON would catch perf regressions before merge.

title: fs_core_trunc_word re-checks four branch conditions per pixel for the truncation guard, serialising what could be a single range check
description: fs_core_trunc_word issues four separate if i < limit / if i+1 < limit / if i+2 < limit / if i+3 < limit checks per pixel. For pixels well inside the limit (the common case for partial frames) these are predictable branches but still consume decode slots. A single if i + 3 < limit guard for the fast path, falling into scalar handling only for the boundary pixel, would reduce branch count by 3× for the bulk.
